high_value_elements: Correctly identifies A-delta fibers (fast, sharp, well-localized pain) and C fibers (slow, dull, aching, poorly localized)
Names substance P and glutamate as key excitatory neurotransmitters at the dorsal horn synapse
Correctly identifies NMDA receptor activation as the mechanism underlying wind-up
Describes central sensitization as increased excitability of dorsal horn neurons following repeated nociceptive input
Names descending inhibitory system components: periaqueductal gray, raphe nucleus/locus coeruleus, dorsolateral funiculus, dorsal horn
major_concerns: Reverses A-delta and C fiber roles
Cannot name any dorsal horn neurotransmitter
Does not know what the NMDA receptor does or why it matters clinically
Cannot locate central sensitization anatomically or explain why it occurs

high_value_elements: Correctly attributes allodynia extending beyond the primary lesion to central sensitization, not just peripheral inflammation
Explains why NSAIDs alone cannot reverse established central sensitization
Correctly selects NMDA antagonist (ketamine or amantadine) for wind-up management with receptor-level rationale
Selects gabapentin based on alpha-2-delta calcium channel subunit mechanism
Applies species-specific neuroanatomic considerations to analgesic selection for non-canine patients
major_concerns: Attributes allodynia cranial to the lesion site to peripheral inflammation rather than central sensitization
Cannot identify a second drug class targeting a different pathway node
Recommends NSAID dose escalation as the primary response to NSAID treatment failure in central sensitization

high_value_elements: Recognizes that allodynia outside the primary lesion territory indicates central rather than peripheral sensitization
Identifies reptile and avian neuroanatomic considerations affecting dosing (thermal biology, metabolic rate)
Prioritizes addressing central sensitization before pain chronifies further
Applies current scientific position that reptiles have functional nociceptive systems warranting analgesia
major_concerns: Treats allodynia outside the lesion territory as peripheral pathology only
States reptiles do not feel pain and analgesia is unnecessary
Cannot distinguish neuropathic from nociceptive pain clinically

high_value_elements: Explains the molecular basis for NMDA antagonist use in wind-up (glutamate binding site blockade)
Connects gabapentin mechanism (alpha-2-delta subunit) to reduction of excitatory neurotransmitter release
Explains why descending inhibition augmentation is logical in central sensitization
Provides mechanism-based client communication about why single-agent therapy fails
major_concerns: Cannot explain why any selected drug acts at the dorsal horn level
Provides only drug names without mechanistic or clinical justification
Cannot connect central sensitization to treatment failure

high_value_elements: Acknowledges that non-mammalian nociceptive systems differ and require species-specific assessment
Recognizes limits of extrapolating mammalian dose guidelines to birds and reptiles
Acknowledges uncertainty about full reversibility of central sensitization
Identifies when neuropathic pain complexity warrants specialist consultation
major_concerns: Applies canine neurophysiology and dosing directly to avian or reptile patients without modification
Claims full certainty about topics where the fellowship explicitly acknowledged uncertainty
Cannot identify any neurophysiologic scenario warranting specialist referral

high_value_elements: Discusses gate control theory and how it informs TENS and physical modality mechanisms
Explains why C fiber blockade by PBM contributes to synergism with pharmacologic analgesics
Discusses serotonin's role in descending modulation and why SNRIs can be useful in neuropathic pain
Connects bradykinin pathophysiology to the rationale for targeted peripheral interventions in early central sensitization
major_concerns: Cannot discuss any nuance in neurophysiology beyond surface-level facts
Treats pain pathways as a simple linear relay without modulation points
Cannot explain why addressing multiple pathway nodes simultaneously is superior to single-target therapy
